request.question: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?

title: Chris Cheney
George Young (rock musician)
Sorry (The Easybeats song)
Black Eyed Bruiser (album)
The Wrights (Australian band)
Wedding Ring (song)
She's So Fine
Evie (song)
Harry Bailey
Hard Road (Stevie Wright album)
sentences: Christopher John Cheney (born 2 January 1975) is an Australian rock musician, record producer and studio owner.
He is the founding mainstay guitarist, songwriter and lead vocalist of the psychobilly band, The Living End, which was formed in 1994 with school mate Scott Owen.

Cheney wrote the group's top 20 hits on the ARIA Singles Chart: "Second Solution" / "Prisoner of Society" (1997), "All Torn Down" (1999), "Pictures in the Mirror" (2000), "Roll On" (2001), "One Said to the Other" (2003), "What's on Your Radio" (2005), "Wake Up" (2006) and "White Noise" (2008).
In 2004 Cheney joined the super group The Wrights which put out a cover version of Stevie Wright's epic 11-minute track, "Evie" as a single.
At the APRA Awards of 2009 Cheney won 'Song of the Year' for writing The Living End's track, "White Noise".
In 2005 he married Emma, the couple have two daughters and are co-owners of a recording facility, Red Door Studios.
In 2011 the Cheney family relocated to Los Angeles.
George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.
Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.

He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).
Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
"Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.
It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.
It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.
Black Eyed Bruiser is the second studio album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album was not as commercially successful as its predecessor "Hard Road" and would be the Wright's final album released with production team Vanda and Young and record label Albert Productions.
The Wrights were a one-off Australian rock music "supergroup".

They consisted of Nic Cester (of Jet), Bernard Fanning (of Powderfinger), Phil Jamieson (of Grinspoon), Kram (of Spiderbait), Chris Cheney (of The Living End), Davey Lane (of You Am I and The Pictures) and Pat Bourke (of Dallas Crane).
They are named after Australian music legend and former Easybeats frontman Stevie Wright, the original performer of the song-trilogy "Evie" which was the group's feature song.
"Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.
"She's So Fine" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached number three in the Australian charts.
"Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.
It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.
It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
Harry Richard Bailey (29 October 1922, Picton, New South Wales – 8 September 1985, Mount White, New South Wales) was an Australian psychiatrist and hospital administrator.

He bore the primary responsibility for treatment of mental patients and drug dependent clients via deep sleep therapy and other method at a mental hospital in the Sydney North Shore suburb of Pennant Hills; he was its principal.
The treatment has been linked with the deaths of a total of 85 patients, including 19 who committed suicide.
Bailey committed suicide while he was investigated.

One of Bailey's clients was Australian rock and pop singer and songwriter Stevie Wright, who was being treated for methadone addiction and later committed suicide, a report that was later part of a 2013 documentary on "Australian Story".
Hard Road is the debut solo album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album's first single "Evie (Part 1)" was hugely successful and the title track was later covered on Rod Stewart's 1974 album "Smiler".
The album itself reach #2 on the Australian albums charts in 1974 was the 16th highest selling album in Australia that year.
The compact disc is currently out-of-print and has become quite rare.
A digital edition was available on iTunes as of June, 2014.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?
hop1_docs: Sorry (The Easybeats song) | "Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.  It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.  It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.

Stevie Wright | Stephen Carlton "Stevie" Wright (20 December 1947 – 27 December 2015), formerly billed as Little Stevie, was an English-born musician and songwriter who has been called Australia's first international pop star.  During 1964–69 he was lead singer of Sydney-based rock and roll band the Easybeats, widely regarded as the greatest Australian pop band of the 1960s.

Kram (musician) | Kram (born Mark Anthony Maher in Finley, New South Wales) is the drummer/singer of Australian band Spiderbait.  His stage name is an anagram of his first name; "Kram" is simply "Mark" spelled backwards.  Kram has appeared at the 2004 benefit concert for the South-East Asian tsunami, WaveAid, as a member of supergroup The Wrights who performed Stevie Wright's 3 part classic, "Evie".

A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song) | "A Place in the Sun" is a 1966 soul single by American and Motown musician Stevie Wonder.  Written by Ronald Miller and Bryan Wells, it was one of Wonder's first songs to contain social commentary.  "A Place in the Sun" was his third Top Ten hit since 1963 and hit #9 on the "Billboard" pop singles chart and # 3 on the R&B charts.  Stevie Wonder also recorded a version of the song in Italian titled "Il Sole è di Tutti" ("The Sun is for Everyone").

George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.

Wedding Ring (song) | "Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.  It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.

Evie (song) | "Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.  It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.  It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
hop1_titles: A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song)
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Sorry" — [1]: "written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright."
- George Young — [5]: "George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946)"; [5]: "Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager,"
- Stevie Wright — [2]: "was an English-born musician and songwriter"

Ambiguities:
- None in the provided passages; George Young is identified as "George Redburn Young" and linked to the Scotland birthplace.

Bridge / missing fact:
- All required fact present and unambiguous: the co-writer George Young's birthplace is given as "Scotland." Final conclusion: George Young was born in Scotland.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "George Young (rock musician)"
- Query: "George Redburn Young born in Scotland"
hop2_docs: George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
Redburn, Northumberland | Redburn is a village in Northumberland, England about 0.5 mi west of Bardon Mill.  It is situated about 4 mi south of Hadrian's Wall.

Redburn | Redburn: His First Voyage is the fourth book by the American writer Herman Melville, first published in London in 1849.  The book is semi-autobiographical and recounts the adventures of a refined youth among coarse and brutal sailors and the seedier areas of Liverpool.  Melville wrote "Redburn" in less than ten weeks.  While one scholar describes it as "arguably his funniest work," scholar F.O. Matthiessen calls it "the most moving of its author's books before "Moby-Dick"".

George Young (Scottish footballer) | George Lewis Young (27 October 1922 – 10 January 1997) was a Scottish footballer, best remembered for his association with Rangers and for being the first player to receive more than 50 caps for the Scotland national team.

George Douglas of Pittendreich | George Douglas of Pittendreich (died 1552) was a member of the powerful Red Douglas family who struggled for control of the young James V of Scotland in 1528.  His second son became James Douglas, 4th Earl of Morton and Regent of Scotland.  Initially, George Douglas promoted the marriage of Mary, Queen of Scots and Prince Edward of England.  After war was declared between England and Scotland he worked for peace and to increase the power of Mary of Guise, the widow of James V.

Rural Municipality of Redburn No. 130 | Redburn No. 130 is a rural municipality in the Canadian province of Saskatchewan, located in SARM Division 2 which is located in Census Division 6.  Southeast of the city of Moose Jaw in the south central region of the province.  The seat of the municipality is Rouleau.

George Strath | George Strath (1 October 1843 – 21 January 1919) was a Scottish professional golfer and golf course designer of the late 19th century and early 20th century.  He was born in St Andrews, Fifeshire, Scotland.  As a young lad he worked as a caddie, carrying the clubs of Old Tom Morris at St Andrews.
response.summary_2: George Young was born in Scotland.
